questions.prompt: What does a Sharpe ratio above 3 typically indicate?

choices.text: Likely overfit. Real-world strategies almost never sustain Sharpe > 3 — even Renaissance's Medallion fund averages ~2 net of fees. When a retail backtest shows Sharpe > 3, the usual cause is overfitting: too many parameters tuned to historical data, lookahead bias hiding in the signal, survivorship bias in the universe, or unrealistic execution assumptions (filled at the inside, zero slippage). Sharpe 1-2 is the realistic range for genuine retail edges. Sharpe > 3 should trigger forensic review — find the bug before celebrating. If after rigorous checks it really is that high, deploy with small size and watch it deflate live (or persist, confirming you found something rare).

questions.explanation: Sharpe > 3 almost always means overfit, lookahead bias, or unrealistic execution. Be suspicious.

questions.prompt: Backtest summary: 18% CAGR, 1.2 Sharpe, 22% max DD. Equity curve shows all gains in 2020-2021. Verdict?

choices.text: Suspicious — the strategy 'worked' during an unusual market regime (COVID-driven momentum and stimulus-fueled trend) but did nothing in normal regimes. Summary stats hide this; the equity curve reveals it. The strategy may have an edge specific to high-volatility / strong-trending environments; it almost certainly doesn't have a generalizable edge across regimes. Diagnostic moves: compute year-by-year returns (need them across regimes, not concentrated), check rolling Sharpe (does it stay positive over time?), test on a different time window (does it work in 2010-2015?). One-window strategies that lit up during a specific regime aren't strategies — they're survivorship. Skip or rebuild with regime-robust testing.

questions.explanation: All gains in one window = regime-specific, not a real edge. Test across diverse periods.
